DLCV Fall 2025 · HW4 · Problem 1

Can Generative Video Models Help Pose Estimation?

DUSt3R · Wide-Baseline Pairs vs. Interpolated Sequences · Public Dataset (40 samples)

Q1 — Model Overview: DUSt3R vs. VGGT

DUSt3R

DUSt3R (Dense and Unconstrained Stereo 3D Reconstruction) is a feed-forward model that directly regresses dense 3D point maps from an image pair without requiring camera intrinsics, depth priors, or explicit feature matching. It is built on a CroCo (Cross-view Completion) ViT encoder-decoder backbone. Given two images, DUSt3R predicts two aligned point maps expressed in the coordinate frame of the first image. For multi-view settings, global alignment is performed via an optimization that brings all pairwise predictions into a common coordinate system, and camera poses are recovered using a PnP solver initialized from a minimum spanning tree.

VGGT (Visual Geometry Grounded Transformer)

VGGT is a more recent feed-forward model that takes an arbitrary number of views and jointly predicts camera parameters (intrinsics and extrinsics), depth maps, and 3D point maps in a single forward pass. Unlike DUSt3R, VGGT does not require any post-hoc optimization or PnP solving; the camera poses are directly regressed. VGGT uses a transformer architecture that attends across all input frames simultaneously, enabling it to reason globally about multi-view geometry from the start.

Key Differences

AspectDUSt3RVGGT
Pose estimationIndirect — via PnP after point map alignmentDirect regression in one forward pass
Multi-view handlingPairwise inference + global alignment optimizationJoint attention over all frames simultaneously
Camera intrinsicsNot required as inputAlso predicted as output
SpeedSlower for many views (O(N²) pairs + optimization)Faster — single forward pass
Wide-baselineStruggles due to limited overlapMore robust due to global reasoning

(2) Case Study — idx 3781 PnP Solver Failure

Input ModeMRE vs. Ground Truth
Pair Input (wide-baseline only)82.886°
Video Input (interpolated sequence)33.350°

The interpolated sequence for idx 3781 is a street scene captured from nearly opposite directions (yaw difference ≈ 80°). When DUSt3R processes the full interpolated sequence, the PnP solver inside init_im_poses.py throws a cv2.error during cv2.solvePnPRansac because the variance of the reconstructed 3D points falls below OpenCV's POINT_VARIANCE_THRESHOLD.

Why does PnP fail? The PnP solver requires the input 3D point set to have sufficient spatial spread. If points are nearly co-planar or co-linear (i.e., all lie on or near a single plane or line), the pose is geometrically under-constrained and the solver cannot reliably recover camera orientation. For street scenes viewed from widely separated angles, the generative interpolation tends to produce blurry or hallucinated intermediate frames that converge to a flat, textureless appearance — collapsing the inferred 3D structure onto a degenerate plane.

Visual Evidence — Interpolated Frames

Fig 1. Selected frames from the DynamiCrafter interpolated sequence for idx 3781. Middle frames show hallucinated/blurry content with little 3D structure — consistent with a co-planar point cloud.

Visual Evidence — Point Clouds

Fig 2. DUSt3R point cloud from pair input (idx 3781) shown from three viewpoints. The structure is nearly co-linear along one axis — a degenerate geometry that causes PnP failure.
Fig 3. DUSt3R point cloud from video (interpolated) input (idx 3781). The structure is still quite flat, explaining the PnP failure, though the final MRE (33.35°) is better than the pair baseline (82.89°).

Q3 — Analysis: Why Does the Performance Gap Exist?

84.4°
MRE — Pair Input
75.4°
MRE — Video Input

The core limitation of DUSt3R on wide-baseline pairs is the lack of visual overlap. DUSt3R relies on cross-view feature matching: it must find corresponding pixels between the two input images. When the baseline is very wide (here, yaw differences of 80–90°), the two views share almost no common scene content, making it impossible to establish reliable correspondences. As a result, the predicted point maps are poorly aligned and the recovered camera poses are highly inaccurate.

Introducing interpolated intermediate frames from a video generative model (DynamiCrafter) addresses this problem by bridging the visual gap. The synthesized frames gradually transition the viewpoint from the first image to the second, ensuring that each consecutive pair of frames shares substantial visual overlap. DUSt3R can then match features across neighboring frames reliably, and the global alignment has access to a chain of consistent pairwise constraints rather than a single degenerate pair.

In summary: the performance improvement comes from converting a wide-baseline stereo problem — which DUSt3R cannot handle — into a short-baseline multi-view problem, which it handles well. The video generative model acts as a geometric bridge, filling in the missing viewpoints and enabling robust feature matching throughout the sequence.

Improvement summary: MRE −9.0° Acc<30° +20pp AUC@30° +0.072 Acc<5° 0% → 2.5%
